guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Dermatology: erythema multiforme (HSV-associated erythema multiforme minor)
DermNet / Australasian College of Dermatologists — erythema multiforme: recognition, target lesions, and distinguishing from Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — topical corticosteroids and symptomatic care for self-limiting inflammatory dermatoses
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance: the source note is framed as an urgent after-hours crisis (sudden, rapidly-spreading burning 'bullseye' eruption spreading from hands to trunk, extreme patient anxiety, telephone triage explicitly raising Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis and severe anaphylaxis), but the scoreable answer is a common, benign, self-limiting erythema multiforme minor triggered by a recent cold sore (HSV). The test is whether the system RESISTS reflexively escalating a distressing-but-benign target-lesion eruption to 000/ED when the source's own red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (no mucosal erosions, no Nikolsky sign, no skin sloughing, no high fever, no airway/anaphylaxis features; Risk Level: Low).

Bidirectional dermatology calibration: erythema multiforme minor is a genuine T1-T2 condition. The correct disposition is to recognise the classic target/iris lesions and the preceding HSV trigger, provide reassurance, advise a mild topical corticosteroid + simple analgesia + cool compresses, and arrange review to lay eyes on the mucosae and confirm the minor variant — NOT urgent same-day ED. Both under-action (dismissive pure self-care with no mucosal safety-net) and over-escalation (T4/T5 / 000) are scored as errors.

Legitimate telehealth-limit safety-net (NOT a baseline uplift): the important-not-to-miss mimic is a severe mucocutaneous reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN — the EM major end of the spectrum), which telehealth cannot fully examine the oral/ocular/genital mucosae or test for Nikolsky sign to exclude. So prominent mandatory escalation edges (mucosal erosions/blistering, high fever, skin peeling in sheets -> T4/T5; facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty -> T5/000) are attached, while the BASELINE stays low.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who inspected and characterised the lesions, performed a careful mucosal (oral/conjunctival/genital) examination, checked for Nikolsky sign and skin sloughing, measured vitals and reconciled medications against the EHR. The AI Doctor cannot examine the skin or mucosae, cannot elicit Nikolsky sign and cannot measure vitals without a device — it is scored on recognising erythema multiforme from history/video, actively screening for the SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis red flags, providing reassurance and patient-performable symptomatic care, and arranging review.
Age (~adult) inferred and flagged — the source note states no age; body weight 76 kg and independent functional status establish an adult.
Sex not stated in the source and inferred with NO firm basis — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-018.
